title: Eugenia confertiflora
taxonomy: Eugenia confertiflora is classified within the family Myrtaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. This family is commonly known as the myrtle family and includes over 3,000 species. Eugenia confertiflora is further classified within the genus Eugenia, which comprises over 600 species of trees and shrubs. The genus Eugenia is native to the tropical and subtropical regions of the world, particularly in the Americas, Africa, and Asia. Eugenia confertiflora is likely to be closely related to other species within the genus Eugenia due to its similar characteristics and geographical distribution.
uses: Eugenia confertiflora, also known as the Brazilian strawberry tree, has been utilized in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's leaves and fruits are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it useful for treating various ailments. In traditional medicine, Eugenia confertiflora is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues, as well as to aid in digestion. The plant's extracts are also thought to have antioxidant and anti-cancer properties, although more research is needed to confirm these claims. Further studies are required to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential applications of Eugenia confertiflora.

The active constituents of Eugenia confertiflora include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its bioactive properties. side_effects: Eugenia confertiflora, also known as the Brazilian cherry or guanabara, is a plant species native to Brazil. intro: Eugenia confertiflora, also known as Brazilian cherry or cumaru, is a tropical plant species native to the Americas, characterized by its bright red fruits and fragrant leaves. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as fever, cough, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Eugenia confertiflora include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Eugenia confertiflora are infusions, tinctures, and decoctions, often used to treat respiratory and gastrointestinal complaints, as well as to promote wound healing.
